name: dumbbell palm rotational bent over row
instructions: Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand with an overhand grip.
Bend forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and your knees slightly bent.
Let your arms hang straight down, palms facing your body.
Engage your core and pull the dumbbells up towards your chest, keeping your elbows close to your body.
As you pull the dumbbells up, rotate your palms so they face away from your body.
Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top of the movement.
Slowly lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position, rotating your palms back to the starting position.
Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

Stand with your feet shoulder-width apart, holding a dumbbell in each hand with an overhand grip. Bend forward at the hips, keeping your back straight and your knees slightly bent. Let your arms hang straight down, palms facing your body. Engage your core and pull the dumbbells up towards your chest, keeping your elbows close to your body. As you pull the dumbbells up, rotate your palms so they face away from your body. Squeeze your shoulder blades together at the top of the movement. Slowly lower the dumbbells back down to the starting position, rotating your palms back to the starting position. Repeat for the desired number of repetitions.

conditionNotes.note: Favor symmetrical loading and supported variations if fatigue changes your posture.
Use pain-free range of motion, slower tempo, and lighter progressions when joints feel irritated.
Weight-bearing resistance exercises are beneficial for bone density when load is appropriate.
Avoid loaded spinal flexion and excessive hinge depth. Brace core and use supported positions.
Maintain consistent breathing throughout. Avoid breath-holding.
Both aerobic and resistance training improve insulin sensitivity. Monitor blood sugar before, during, and after sessions. Have a fast-acting carb available.
Rhythm-based exercises and large-amplitude movements are beneficial. Focus on motor confidence.
Focus on muscle co-contraction and joint stability. Avoid locking out joints passively.
Functional patterns are generally safe. Prioritize pelvic floor awareness and avoid Valsalva.
Start light with higher reps and shorter ROM. Progress load gradually as pain-free capacity increases.
